ciso_simulation.answer: Only the named product, security, privacy, and clinical owners can approve release scope changes; buyer-specific answers must be attached to this packet before promotion.
ciso_simulation.question: Who can approve a release when buyer review questions change?

ciso_simulation.answer: It drafts support text for prior-auth workflows, but all denials, approvals, and outbound member decisions stay under human review in payer systems.

ciso_simulation.answer: No. The runtime exposes only read-only retrieval for policy context, and every workflow still routes through a human reviewer before downstream payer action.
ciso_simulation.question: Can the model call tools or external systems without a reviewer?

ciso_simulation.answer: Artifact 02 names the model, permissions, and disallowed uses, and the CHAI crosswalk maps those fields to the question structure many healthcare buyers already use.
ciso_simulation.question: How do you translate this into a buyer questionnaire format?

gaps.compensating_control: High-risk prompts are blocked by policy middleware, privileged tools require human approval, and production traffic is rate-limited with manual escalation.
gaps.description: ClaraHealth has completed internal prompt-injection and jailbreak testing, but the scheduled external red-team exercise has not yet run against the June 2026 release candidate.
gaps.remediation: Complete the contracted third-party red-team, document findings, and append the signed report addendum to Artifact 02 before production expansion.
gaps.title: Independent adversarial red-team not yet complete

ciso_simulation.answer: No. ClaraHealth contractually disables provider training use, limits runtime data to the active workflow, and documents this control in the vendor due-diligence record.

ciso_simulation.answer: Material dependency changes trigger a vendor review and update to this artifact before the affected release can be promoted.
ciso_simulation.question: How are third-party changes surfaced to buyers?

ciso_simulation.answer: Break-glass access is approved separately, expires automatically after four hours, and is reviewed in the next weekly control meeting with security leadership.
ciso_simulation.question: If support needs emergency access, what stops it from becoming permanent?

ciso_simulation.answer: No. Tenant identifiers scope retrieval, document access, and export jobs, and those boundaries are covered in application tests and release review.
ciso_simulation.question: Can one tenant ever retrieve another tenant's packet inputs?

gaps.compensating_control: Break-glass access is time-boxed to four hours, paged to security leadership, and reviewed at the next weekly change-control meeting.
gaps.description: Emergency administrator access exists for incident response, but recertification evidence is maintained in an operations runbook rather than enforced by automated IAM review workflows.
gaps.remediation: Move the break-glass role into the quarterly access-review campaign and archive reviewer approvals in the packet evidence store.
gaps.title: Quarterly break-glass recertification is still manual

fields.value: SAML SSO with enforced MFA for all workforce users
Least-privilege reviewer, manager, support, and security roles
Logical tenant partitioning with policy and record scoping
Idle timeout, IP anomaly alerts, and device checks
Time-boxed emergency role with executive notification
Security-engineering only; dual approval required
Manual review evidence tracked in ops runbook

ciso_simulation.answer: Working data is retained only for the contracted window, deleted on the defined SLA, and customer exports are logged so the buyer can audit what was generated.
ciso_simulation.question: What happens to PHI after a packet is delivered?

ciso_simulation.answer: The primary systems delete on time today; the current gap is collecting the signed attestation from one subprocessor so the evidence chain is complete.
ciso_simulation.question: How do you handle deletion evidence from subprocessors?

gaps.compensating_control: Exports to the analytics vendor are tokenized, encrypted, and excluded from new customers until the attestation backlog is cleared.
gaps.description: Customer deletion requests are executed in ClaraHealth systems on time, but signed deletion attestations from one analytics subprocessor have not yet been returned for the May cycle.
gaps.remediation: Collect the missing signed attestations, attach them to the retention packet, and update the subprocessor scorecard with a hard SLA.
gaps.title: Subprocessor deletion attestations are pending for the last retention cycle

AES-256 at rest with cloud KMS-managed keys

30 days unless contract requires shorter retention
Seven calendar days after verified request or contract termination

ciso_simulation.answer: The workflow enforces human review and logs the reviewer who accepted, edited, or rejected the draft before anything leaves the platform.
ciso_simulation.question: What stops generated text from reaching a payer untouched?

ciso_simulation.answer: High-risk categories route to mandatory clinical escalation and cannot be cleared by a non-clinical reviewer.
ciso_simulation.question: How do you handle unsafe medical nuance?

Required for contraindications, oncology, and pediatric edge cases
Reviewers can reject or fully rewrite generated output
Initial onboarding plus annual refresh with signed attestation
Reviewer identity, timestamp, and final disposition captured
Weekly QA review of approved and rejected drafts
Security or clinical escalation within one business hour

ciso_simulation.answer: No. Tool access is allowlisted at the runtime boundary, and the model has no authority to create new actions outside that contract.
ciso_simulation.question: Can a prompt injection make the system call an unapproved tool?

ciso_simulation.answer: Prompts are versioned in git, reviewed like code, and tied to release approvals so the exact prompt set is attributable to each packet version.
ciso_simulation.question: How are prompt changes kept from drifting quietly over time?

fields.value: Versioned repository with protected branches
Security and product review required for policy changes
Pre- and post-generation policy checks with deny rules
Schema checks plus citation-presence validation
Read-only retrieval and packet export only
No arbitrary egress from runtime sandbox
Server-side secret store only; none exposed in buyer packet pages

ciso_simulation.answer: Buyers receive the initial notice within the contractual window, the scoped impact summary, containment actions, and the named follow-up owner from incident command.
ciso_simulation.question: What does a buyer get if there is a confirmed security incident?

ciso_simulation.answer: Yes. Abuse, policy-violation, export, and model-guardrail events are tagged separately so the responder can isolate AI workflow issues quickly.
ciso_simulation.question: Are AI-specific alerts separate from standard SaaS alerts?

fields.value: Auth, admin, packet export, and model safety events stream to SIEM
24x7 security and infrastructure rotation
Severity matrix with named incident commander and comms owner
Initial notice within 24 hours for confirmed material incidents
Semiannual cross-functional tabletop exercises
Relevant logs preserved for at least 12 months
Written review with tracked action items and owners

ciso_simulation.answer: The previous signed bundle stays pinned and ready; the remaining gap is rehearsing the exact rollback on the June bundle so the timing evidence is fresh.
ciso_simulation.question: If the new bundle regresses, how fast can you unwind it?

ciso_simulation.answer: Because the affected workflow remains in mandatory human review, and broader rollout is blocked until the evaluation set reaches the defined threshold.
ciso_simulation.question: Why release at all if oncology coverage is incomplete?

gaps.compensating_control: Production deployments remain canary-only with a frozen fallback bundle and a manual release commander required for promotion.
gaps.description: Release gating includes a documented rollback path, but the exact rollback drill for the current bundle version has not been rehearsed end to end since the May dependency update.
gaps.remediation: Run the rollback drill in the staging clone, capture timing and checkpoints, and attach the signed drill record to Artifact 08.
gaps.title: Rollback drill has not been executed on the current model bundle

gaps.compensating_control: Oncology cases remain in mandatory human-review mode and are excluded from straight-through automation decisions.
gaps.description: The regression suite covers the top payer workflows, but oncology-denial examples are still below the target sample count required by ClaraHealth's release rubric.
gaps.remediation: Expand the labeled evaluation set with the queued oncology cases and rerun acceptance thresholds before broader rollout to commercial plans.
gaps.title: Shadow-mode evaluation set is below target coverage for oncology denials

ciso_simulation.answer: The system records the request metadata, triggered guardrails, blocked tools, reviewer actions, and the final disposition so investigators can reconstruct the event.
ciso_simulation.question: What gets logged when a suspicious prompt arrives?

ciso_simulation.answer: The heuristics are live today; the current open item is the formal quarterly review and sign-off for the refreshed taxonomy.
ciso_simulation.question: How do you keep abuse rules current as attacker patterns shift?

gaps.compensating_control: Existing abuse heuristics, rate limits, and manual analyst review remain active and alert to the on-call security engineer.
gaps.description: Monitoring rules are active, but the formal quarterly review that refreshes abuse patterns, blocked intents, and reviewer guidance has not yet been signed for Q2 2026.
gaps.remediation: Hold the review with security and clinical-ops leads, publish the updated abuse taxonomy, and attach the sign-off minutes to Artifact 09.
gaps.title: Prompt-abuse taxonomy review is overdue for the current quarter

fields.value: Prompt metadata, tool calls, reviewer actions, exports, and admin changes
PHI minimized and tokenized before long-term storage
365 days online, 18 months archived

Prompt-injection, credential capture, policy circumvention, and data exfiltration patterns
Per-tenant throttles plus anomaly-based lockouts

ciso_simulation.answer: Backups, restore tests, and the failover runbook are complete; the only open item is the joint tabletop on the new secondary-region path so the communications chain is current.
ciso_simulation.question: What evidence do you have that you can recover the service?

ciso_simulation.answer: Packet review remains available from the warm standby, with manual traffic cutover and predefined buyer communications led by the infrastructure director.
ciso_simulation.question: What is the buyer impact if failover is needed?

gaps.compensating_control: Nightly immutable backups, warm standby services, and manual traffic cutover runbooks are already in place and tested separately.
gaps.description: The platform meets backup and restore objectives, but the tabletop covering the newly introduced secondary-region inference path has not yet been completed with vendor participation.
gaps.remediation: Run the multi-party tabletop, document decision owners and communications timing, and store the signed action log in Artifact 10.
gaps.title: Disaster recovery tabletop with the newest cloud failover path is pending

Quarterly backup restore validation complete

explicit_non_claims: This packet does not claim that the model is error-free or clinically deterministic.
This packet does not replace buyer-specific legal, privacy, or procurement review.
This packet does not claim autonomous decision-making authority for claims approval or denial.
attestation.statement: I attest that this packet accurately describes ClaraHealth prior-auth-v2.1 as deployed for buyer review on June 10, 2026; cited controls are implemented as stated, and every known material gap is explicitly disclosed with an owner, target date, and compensating control.

chai_crosswalk.chai_field: Training data and data use disclosures
Identity, access, and tenant isolation controls

Artifacts 08 and 09 -> Release Gates / Abuse Controls

chai_crosswalk.chai_field: Incident response, continuity, and buyer verification
chai_crosswalk.packet_location: Artifacts 11, 10, and 12 -> Detection / Recovery / Verification Assets

executive_summary.deal_context: Prepared for a healthcare-plan security review where the buyer requested AI-specific evidence beyond SOC 2 and standard vendor due-diligence materials.
executive_summary.status_line: Twelve attested artifacts are included. Seven open gaps remain, each explicitly owned, dated, and bounded by a compensating control that is live today.
executive_summary.system_description: ClaraHealth prior-auth-v2.1 drafts payer-facing prior-authorization rationale for healthcare revenue-cycle teams. The system retrieves plan policies, structures reviewer notes, and produces a cited draft for human approval; it does not autonomously approve, deny, or transmit payer decisions.
top_gaps: G-01 - External adversarial red-team is scheduled but not yet complete.
G-04 - Current-bundle rollback drill has not been rehearsed end to end.
G-05 - Oncology shadow-mode evaluation coverage is below target and remains gated behind human review.

owasp_llm_assessment.control: Guardrails, tool allowlists, and human review before any buyer-facing action.
Schema validation, citation checks, and reviewer approval before export.
No customer-data training and versioned runtime corpus approvals.
Per-tenant throttles, queue controls, and operational fallback to manual review.
Annual vendor diligence plus material-change review for core providers.
PHI minimization, encryption, retention controls, and deletion workflows.
Read-only retrieval tools only; no arbitrary plugin execution.
No autonomous approvals, denials, or external submissions.
Mandatory human review, clinical escalation queues, and quality sampling.
Vendor-managed API, secret isolation, and restricted runtime egress.
